# Spatial Regression {#spatial_regression}

## Introduction

![](images/banner-spatial-regression.png)

### Aims {.unnumbered}

The aims of this practical are to:

1.  Understand how spatial regression is different from classical regression and why it matters. 
2.  Understand the differences between common forms of spatial regression.
3.  Use spatial regression to reveal relationships in a sociological context. 

### Application {.unnumbered}

To that end, we'll apply non-spatial and spatial regression to predict life expectancy at the MSOA-level ([Middle Layer Super Output Area ](https://www.ons.gov.uk/methodology/geography/ukgeographies/statisticalgeographies)) in Greater Manchester.

### Data {#sr_data .unnumbered}

The data for this practical are sourced from the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC, [Fingertips](https://fingertips.phe.org.uk/)) and the Office for National Statistics (ONS), and are available in [`data/dhsc`] and [`data/ons/msoa`] respectively. Key attributes have been retained and are described below:

-   ONS MSOA-level geometry [[Source](https://geoportal.statistics.gov.uk/datasets/6b282db29762450881ed5159259a6e4e_0/explore?location=52.837634%2C-2.489483%2C6)]
    -   `fuel_poverty`: the percentage of households in the corresponding geographical area that were in fuel poverty.
    -   `household_poverty`: the number of households (modelled) in fuel poverty.
    -   `household_total`: the total number of households.
-   ONS Economic Activity Status [[Source](https://www.ons.gov.uk/datasets/create/filter-outputs/20ce9188-476a-4e89-af57-0285e69f7098#get-data)]
  - !!
  - !!
-   ONS Highest level of qualification [[Source](https://www.ons.gov.uk/datasets/create/filter-outputs/2a85d026-ea61-492f-9b43-117b66a3af4e#get-data)]
  - !!
  - !!
-   ONS Disability [[Source](https://www.ons.gov.uk/datasets/create/filter-outputs/0248ce8b-df1d-413f-a724-2d62feed9556#get-data)]
  - !!
  - !!
-   ONS Household income [[Source](https://www.ons.gov.uk/employmentandlabourmarket/peopleinwork/earningsandworkinghours/datasets/smallareaincomeestimatesformiddlelayersuperoutputareasenglandandwales)]
  - !!
  - !!
-   DHSC Index of Multiple Deprivation [[Source](https://fingertips.phe.org.uk/profile/local-health/data#page/0)]
  - !!
  - !!
-   DHSC Fuel poverty [[Source](https://fingertips.phe.org.uk/profile/local-health/data#page/0)]
  - !!
  - !!

### Tools {.unnumbered}

[Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/ordinary-least-squares.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Statistics), [Spatial Autoregression](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/spatial-autoregression.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Statistics)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Practical

During this practical...

[1] Load MSOA geometries and check / set map projection (British National Grid, CRS 27700)

[2] Filter to region of interest (north-west, n=932) -\> Add lookup table (csv) -\> Add permanent join (MSOA21CD) -\> Select by Attributes = MSOA_2021_lookup_table.csv.RGN22NM = 'North West' -\> Export Selection (this retains Join fields) -\> Alternatively (keeping the features selected), remove the join, and then export -\> Remove lookup table

[3] Add ONS and DHSC data (csv, n=7)

[4] Table Joins (MSOA21CD to msoa_code) - life expectancy

[5] Assess spatial autocorrelation (definitely) and look at spatial weights?

[6] Prepare for OLS (unique ID field) -\> Calculate field ('unique_id') -\> Long integer (32-bit) -\> Sequential Number

[7] OLS -\> Inspect output (pdf) and residuals -\> Life Expectancy and Income: R2 = 0.621306

[8] Check for spatial autocorrelation in the OLS residuals -\> Global Moran's I: Clustered (z-score of 14.142665, less than 1% likelihood that this clustered pattern could be the result of random chance) -\> LISA: HH clusters in Cumbria, LL in Manchester

[9] Spatial Autoregression -\> SEM -\> SLM

[10] Check for autocorrelation again? -\> Still autocorrelated... -\> Same pattern with LISA...

--------------- Data Wrangling:

-   Disability: pivoted, renamed columns
-   Economic Activity Status: pivoted, renamed columns, and simplified to: -\> employment_dna -\> employed_not_ftstudent -\> employed_and_ftstudent -\> economically_inactive_not_ftstudent\
    -\> economically_inactive_and_ftstudent
-   Highest Level of Qualification: pivoted, renamed columns to: -\> qualification_dna\
    -\> no_qualifications -\> level_one -\> level_two -\> level_three -\> level_four -\> apprenticeships_or_similar
-   Household Income: converted from xls, retained total_annual_income only, filtered to north-west
-   Life Expectancy: -\> Removed many columns and renamed -\> Pivoted, retaining male and female life expectancy, plus confidence intervals -\> Two attempts, as saved the wrong MSOA code
-   IMD: remove and renamed columns (no pivot)
-   Fuel Poverty: remove and renamed columns (no pivot). Retained: -\> household_poverty: The number of households in the corresponding geographical area that were in fuel poverty (modelled) -\> household_total: [presumably] the total number of households in the MSOA

## Extra

## Resources

- Anselin, L. ([1988](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-94-015-7799-1)). Spatial econometrics: methods and models. Kluwer Academic Publishers google schola, 2, pp.283-291.
- Anselin, L. ([2002](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1574-0862.2002.tb00120.x)). Under the hood issues in the specification and interpretation of spatial regression models. Agricultural economics, 27(3), pp.247-267.
- Gibbons, S. and Overman, H.G. ([2012](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9787.2012.00760.x)). Mostly pointless spatial econometrics?. Journal of regional Science, 52(2), pp.172-191.
